refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) does not exceed the customer-defined E_target over the agreed evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across the range of supported worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 as stated is a KPI support requirement but lacks specificity on measurement scope, boundary conditions, and operational context. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is a measurable outcome dependent on operator behavior, vehicle efficiency, and system-level optimization. The requirement now explicitly bounds the evaluation period and scenario scope, making it verifiable and traceable to design decisions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Establish baseline energy consumption and ton-moved metrics during controlled field trials. Instrument the vehicle with energy sensors (fuel/battery consumption) and load/distance sensors. Collect data across representative worksite scenarios (terrain, load profiles, operator skill levels). Calculate energy_per_ton for each scenario and aggregate over the evaluation period. Compare against E_target with statistical confidence bounds.
sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The vehicle shall minimize energy consumption per unit work output through efficient drivetrain, hydraulics, and engine management.
FR-EE-002: The system shall provide real-time energy consumption feedback to the operator to enable informed operational decisions.
FR-EE-003: The teleoperation system shall minimize latency and control losses to prevent operator-induced energy waste.
FR-EE-004: The system shall log and aggregate energy and productivity metrics to enable post-mission energy efficiency analysis.
FR-EE-005: Safety systems shall not degrade energy efficiency through unnecessary energy-consuming safeguards or redundancy.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a closed-loop energy management system with real-time feedback, predictive load modeling, and operator coaching to optimize energy per ton.
DP-B: Optimize vehicle powertrain efficiency (engine tuning, hydraulic efficiency, drivetrain losses) and rely on operator training and worksite planning to achieve energy targets.

DP-C: Implement a hybrid approach combining vehicle-level efficiency optimization with system-level telemetry collection, operator feedback, and post-mission analytics to identify and correct energy waste patterns.
DP-D: Mandate energy-efficient operational procedures through safety interlocks and automated load limiting, accepting reduced productivity to guarantee energy targets.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Implement a hybrid approach combining vehicle-level efficiency optimization (powertrain tuning, hydraulic efficiency) with system-level telemetry collection, real-time operator feedback via dashboard, and post-mission analytics to identify and correct energy waste patterns. This DP balances energy efficiency with operational flexibility and safety.

potential_sources: Energy feedback display (DP-C) couples to operator interface design and telemetry collection architecture.
Post-mission analytics couples to data logging, network bandwidth, and backend analytics infrastructure.
Vehicle powertrain optimization couples to engine control unit (ECU) and hydraulic system design.
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are manageable through clear interface definitions (Telemetry for data flow, Tasking for operator commands). No circular dependencies detected. DP-C avoids over-constraint (DP-D) while maintaining measurable outcomes (DP-A/B lack system-level visibility).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Operator will respond to real-time energy feedback and modify behavior to improve efficiency. Risk: Operator may ignore feedback or prioritize speed over efficiency.
Assumption: Vehicle powertrain can be optimized without compromising safety or reliability. Risk: Aggressive tuning may increase wear or reduce component life.
Assumption: Telemetry infrastructure (sensors, network, logging) is reliable and has sufficient bandwidth. Risk: Data loss or latency may corrupt energy calculations.
Assumption: Energy targets are achievable with the selected vehicle platform. Risk: Customer expectations may exceed physical capabilities.
Assumption: Post-mission analytics can identify actionable patterns. Risk: Insufficient data granularity or operator variability may obscure root causes.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify: (1) energy feedback update frequency (e.g., 1 Hz), (2) analytics granularity (per-task, per-operator, per-scenario), (3) feedback modality (visual, auditory, haptic), and (4) thresholds for alerting operators to energy-inefficient behavior. Add a requirement that vehicle powertrain optimization shall not reduce safety margins or component reliability.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution IM is the top-level system boundary encompassing all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and their integration. At this decomposition level, the energy efficiency KPI is a system-level property that emerges from the coordinated behavior of vehicle efficiency, operator feedback, and analytics. The NGHE Solution IM owns the energy efficiency measurement framework, cross-subsystem telemetry aggregation, and post-mission analytics pipeline.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Energy consumption (kWh or fuel volume), ton-moved (load × distance), operator actions (throttle, bucket position), vehicle state (RPM, hydraulic pressure, temperature). Flows from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to NGHE Solution analytics backend and operator dashboard.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Customer-defined energy targets (E_target), evaluation period parameters, worksite scenario definitions, operator assignments. Flows from Site Manager / Project Manager to NGHE Solution for configuration and baseline setting.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Energy-related safety alerts (e.g., overheating due to inefficient operation, low fuel warnings). Flows between Safety Services and NGHE Solution analytics to ensure energy optimization does not compromise safety.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Connectivity for telemetry transmission, remote analytics, and operator feedback updates. Required for real-time energy feedback and post-mission data synchronization.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level container for the energy efficiency KPI. It owns the measurement framework, cross-subsystem telemetry aggregation, analytics, and operator feedback. Minimal diffusion: only the Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems contribute data; Safety Services provides constraints. This is the correct architectural level for this FR.

Vehicle owns powertrain efficiency optimization (DP-C component 1) but does not own operator feedback, analytics, or cross-system measurement. Assigning the entire FR to Vehicle would fragment the energy efficiency concern across subsystems and lose system-level visibility. Vehicle is a component, not the system.

Teleoperation owns operator feedback mechanisms and remote control efficiency but does not own vehicle powertrain optimization or post-mission analytics. Assigning the FR to Teleoperation would create high diffusion and miss the vehicle-level efficiency gains. Teleoperation is a subsystem, not the system.

Safety Services provides constraints on energy optimization (e.g., no unsafe efficiency measures) but does not own the energy efficiency mechanism itself. Assigning the FR to Safety Services would be a category error and create severe diffusion. Safety Services is a cross-cutting concern, not the primary owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The energy efficiency KPI (FR-NGHE-003) is a system-level emergent property that requires coordinated action across Vehicle (powertrain), Teleoperation (operator feedback), and Safety Services (constraint enforcement). The NGHE Solution IM is the appropriate boundary because it owns the measurement framework, telemetry aggregation, analytics, and feedback loop that tie these subsystems together. Assigning the FR to any single subsystem would fragment the concern and lose system-level visibility.
containment_risks: Risk: Vehicle subsystem may optimize powertrain efficiency in isolation without considering operator feedback or analytics, leading to suboptimal system-level energy performance.
Risk: Teleoperation subsystem may provide energy feedback without coordination with vehicle efficiency measures, creating conflicting optimization signals.
Risk: Analytics backend may lack real-time access to vehicle telemetry, delaying feedback and reducing operator responsiveness.
Risk: Safety Services constraints may not be properly integrated into energy optimization logic, leading to unsafe efficiency measures.
Risk: Measurement boundary may not align with customer evaluation period or scenario definitions, invalidating energy_per_ton calculations.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish explicit interface contracts between NGHE Solution and Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. Define: (1) Telemetry schema and update frequency, (2) energy target configuration protocol (Tasking), (3) safety constraint propagation (SafetyAlert), and (4) operator feedback format and latency requirements. Create a system-level energy efficiency control loop that coordinates subsystem actions and validates that energy_per_ton remains within E_target bounds.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR clearly specifies the energy efficiency outcome (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) and the evaluation context (evaluation period, nominal conditions, supported scenarios). DP-C (hybrid vehicle optimization + telemetry + analytics + feedback) directly addresses this outcome by combining vehicle-level efficiency with system-level visibility and operator engagement. The DP is neither over-constrained nor under-specified; it provides a logical solution path without prescribing implementation details. Coupling is manageable through well-defined interfaces.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: Vehicle powertrain optimization (DP-C component 1) is owned by Vehicle subsystem, not NGHE Solution. NGHE Solution must establish clear interface contracts (e.g., via Telemetry) to receive efficiency metrics and validate that vehicle-level optimization is occurring.

Diffusion Risk 2: Operator feedback mechanism (DP-C component 2) is owned by Teleoperation subsystem. NGHE Solution must define the feedback format, latency requirements, and integration with analytics to ensure coherent operator experience.

Diffusion Risk 3: Post-mission analytics (DP-C component 3) may require backend infrastructure (database, compute) not owned by NGHE Solution core. Clear ownership and interface definitions are required.
Diffusion Risk 4: Safety constraints (DP-C implicit) must be enforced by Safety Services. NGHE Solution must receive SafetyAlert signals and incorporate them into energy optimization logic.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution IM appropriately contains the energy efficiency measurement framework, telemetry aggregation, analytics orchestration, and feedback coordination. Subsystem-level implementations (Vehicle powertrain, Teleoperation UI, Safety Services constraints) are owned by their respective modules but coordinated through NGHE Solution interfaces. This is a healthy hierarchical decomposition with clear boundaries.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible through field trials with instrumented vehicles. Energy consumption and ton-moved metrics can be collected via Telemetry interface. Post-mission analytics can aggregate data over the evaluation period and compare against E_target. Operator feedback effectiveness can be assessed through A/B testing (with/without feedback). Safety compliance can be verified through Safety Services integration tests.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The FR is clearly allocated to NGHE Solution IM with sub-allocations to Vehicle (powertrain efficiency), Teleoperation (operator feedback), and Safety Services (constraint enforcement). Each subsystem has a defined role in achieving the energy efficiency outcome. The measurement framework and analytics are owned by NGHE Solution, ensuring system-level visibility and accountability.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Establish a System Energy Efficiency Control Board (SECB) to coordinate Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. SECB owns the energy efficiency measurement framework, analytics, and feedback loop.

Revision 2: Define explicit interface contracts for Telemetry (energy consumption, ton-moved, operator actions), Tasking (E_target, evaluation period), SafetyAlert (energy-related safety constraints), and Network Link (bandwidth, latency requirements).

Revision 3: Create a detailed energy efficiency measurement plan specifying: (a) sensor types and accuracy requirements, (b) data collection frequency and granularity, (c) aggregation logic for energy_per_ton calculation, (d) statistical methods for comparing against E_target, and (e) confidence bounds and acceptance criteria.

Revision 4: Define operator feedback requirements: (a) real-time energy consumption display, (b) efficiency alerts (e.g., 'operating above target'), (c) post-mission energy report, and (d) feedback latency and modality (visual, auditory, haptic).

Revision 5: Establish safety guardrails: (a) energy optimization shall not reduce safety margins, (b) SafetyAlert signals shall override energy optimization, (c) safety-critical systems shall not be disabled for efficiency gains.

decision.justification: The refined FR-NGHE-003 is not atomic; it encompasses multiple concerns: vehicle powertrain efficiency, operator feedback, telemetry collection, analytics, and safety integration. The selected DP-C (hybrid approach) is logically sound and well-coupled to the FR. The assigned IM (NGHE Solution) is the correct architectural boundary for system-level energy efficiency. However, the FR requires further decomposition into sub-FRs to allocate responsibilities to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems and to define the measurement framework and analytics pipeline. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify ownership, interface contracts, and verification strategies.
decision.justification_detail: The gate review identified four diffusion risks related to subsystem coordination (Vehicle powertrain, Teleoperation feedback, Safety Services constraints, backend analytics). These risks are manageable but require explicit sub-FR decomposition to establish clear ownership and interface contracts. The sub-FRs identified in the refined_fr section (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-005) provide a roadmap for the next level of decomposition.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The vehicle shall minimize energy consumption per unit work output through efficient drivetrain, hydraulics, and engine management.
FR-EE-002: The system shall provide real-time energy consumption feedback to the operator to enable informed operational decisions.
FR-EE-003: The teleoperation system shall minimize latency and control losses to prevent operator-induced energy waste.
FR-EE-004: The system shall log and aggregate energy and productivity metrics to enable post-mission energy efficiency analysis.
FR-EE-005: Safety systems shall not degrade energy efficiency through unnecessary energy-consuming safeguards or redundancy.

assumptions: Assumption A1: Customer-defined E_target is achievable with the selected vehicle platform and operational procedures.
Assumption A2: Operator will respond to real-time energy feedback and modify behavior to improve efficiency.
Assumption A3: Vehicle powertrain can be optimized without compromising safety, reliability, or component life.
Assumption A4: Telemetry infrastructure (sensors, network, logging) is reliable and has sufficient bandwidth for real-time feedback.
Assumption A5: Post-mission analytics can identify actionable patterns from energy and productivity data.
Assumption A6: Safety Services constraints can be integrated into energy optimization logic without creating conflicts.
Assumption A7: Evaluation period and scenario definitions are stable and well-understood by all stakeholders.
open_risks: Risk R1: Operator may ignore energy feedback or prioritize speed/productivity over efficiency, limiting effectiveness of DP-C.
Risk R2: Vehicle powertrain optimization may increase wear or reduce component life, creating maintenance cost trade-offs.
Risk R3: Telemetry data loss or latency may corrupt energy_per_ton calculations and invalidate comparisons against E_target.
Risk R4: Safety constraints may conflict with energy optimization, requiring trade-off decisions that reduce efficiency gains.
Risk R5: Customer expectations for E_target may exceed physical capabilities of the vehicle platform.
Risk R6: Subsystem-level implementations (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) may not coordinate effectively, leading to suboptimal system-level energy performance.
Risk R7: Analytics backend may lack real-time access to vehicle telemetry, delaying feedback and reducing operator responsiveness.
evidence_needed: Evidence E1: Field trial data demonstrating energy_per_ton performance across representative worksite scenarios.
Evidence E2: Operator feedback effectiveness study (A/B testing with/without real-time energy feedback).
Evidence E3: Vehicle powertrain optimization validation (efficiency gains, reliability impact, component wear analysis).
Evidence E4: Telemetry infrastructure performance testing (sensor accuracy, network bandwidth, data loss rates).
Evidence E5: Safety Services integration testing (constraint propagation, conflict resolution, safety margin preservation).
Evidence E6: Analytics algorithm validation (energy_per_ton calculation accuracy, pattern detection effectiveness).
Evidence E7: Stakeholder acceptance of energy targets and evaluation methodology (Site Manager, Equipment Operator, Safety Manager).
